result.content:
FINAL_ANSWER: F(x) = (n-1) k_B T(x) / x, where T(x) = T_0 (x_0/x)^{2/3} (for a 3D chain, or more generally T(x) scales such that S is constant)

Actually, for a freely jointed chain in the Gaussian limit, the internal energy is U = 3/2 n k_B T. The entropy is S = n k_B [ln(Z/n) + 3/2]. For a Gaussian chain, Z(T, x) ∝ T^{3n/2} x^{-(n-1)}. 
To keep S constant, T^{3n/2} x^{-(n-1)} = const ⇒ T ∝ x^{(n-1)/(3n/2)} ∝ x^{2/3} (for large n).

The adiabatic force is F = (dU/dx)_S. 
U = 3/2 n k_B T(x). 
F = 3/2 n k_B (dT/dx)_S.
Since T(x) = C x^{2/3}, dT/dx = (2/3) C x^{-1/3} = (2/3) T/x.
F = 3/2 n k_B (2/3 T/x) = n k_B T(x) / x.

More precisely, for the Gaussian model: 
F(x) = (n-1) k_B T(x) / x

JUSTIFICATION: For a thermally isolated (adiabatic) process, the entropy S must remain constant. For a freely jointed chain (Gaussian approximation), the partition function scales as Z ∝ T^{3n/2} x^{-(n-1)}. Setting dS = 0 implies T^{3n/2} x^{-(n-1)} = ∝ constant, which gives the adiabatic temperature scaling T(x) ∝ x^{(n-1)/(3n/2)}. The adiabatic force is calculated via the thermodynamic relation F = (dU/dx)_S. Given U = 3/2 n k_B T, substituting the adiabatic temperature dependence yields the force law F(x) proportional to T(x)/x.
